428	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
429	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
430	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle,
431	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₁₂,
432	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₂₃, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₁,
433	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₂, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₃,
434	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.quasiIso_horseshoeι, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.single₀_hom_ext}
435	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
436	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
437	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
438	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
439	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
440	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
445	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
446	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
447	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
448	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
449	  cochain complexes
450	  \[
451	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
452	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
453	  \]
454	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
455	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
456	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
457	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
458	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
459	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
460	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
461	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
462	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
463	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
464	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
465	\end{proof}

467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
470	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
471	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
472	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
474	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
685	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
686	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
687	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
689	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
690	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
692	  % between abelian categories and assume
693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
694	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
695	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
696	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
697	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence

701	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
710	  \[
711	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
712	  \]
713	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
714	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).

716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.
748	\end{proof}

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}

813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}

865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by

1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)

1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)

1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})

1033	  \]

998	  precursor and an unconditional ladder transport.
999	  \begin{itemize}

1001	      is pure homological algebra: \emph{given}
1002	      \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of two
1003	      consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
1004	      \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\), the homology of the section complex at degree
1005	      \(q+1\) is zero. It chains
1006	      \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\),
1007	      \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.exactAt\_iff'}\) (to expose the short complex
1008	      around the node), and the abelian-group exactness criterion
1009	      \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff\_function\_exact}\) (which states that
1010	      a short complex of abelian groups is exact iff for every \(x_2\) with
1011	      \(g(x_2) = 0\) there is \(x_1\) with \(f(x_1) = x_2\)).
1012	    \item The unconditional \(\operatorname{sectionCechAbExact}\) discharges that
1013	      hypothesis by transporting the already-proved \(R\)-module exactness
1014	      \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
1015	      Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the degreewise additive
1016	      isomorphism, using the ladder-transport lemma

1021	\begin{proof}
1022	  \uses{lem:section_cech_objd_apply, lem:section_cech_product_equiv,
1023	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1024	  Fix \(p = q + 1 \geq 1\). By the precursor
1025	  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_isZero\_homology\_of\_objD\_exact}\) it suffices to
1026	  exhibit \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying group homomorphisms of the
1027	  two consecutive coface differentials \(\operatorname{objD} q\),
1028	  \(\operatorname{objD}(q+1)\) of the section complex.
1029	
1030	  This exactness is obtained by the ladder transport
1031	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact.of\_ladder\_addEquiv\_of\_exact}\). The vertical maps
1032	  of the ladder are the degreewise additive isomorphisms
1033	  \(e_p = \prod_\sigma \varphi_\sigma \circ \operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_p\):
1034	  the product equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv} followed by the
1035	  coordinatewise comparison \(\varphi_\sigma\) identifying each section group with the
1036	  localised module \(M_{s_\sigma}\). This composite vertical map is the additive
1037	  equivalence \(\operatorname{sectionToModuleAddEquiv}\), whose \(\sigma\)-component is
1038	  \(\operatorname{sectionToModuleAddEquiv\_apply}\) (the forward coordinate formula) and
1039	  whose inverse-direction coordinate is \(\operatorname{sectionProdEquiv\_symm\_apply}\). The two commuting squares of the ladder are the
1040	  coface match: the abstract unfold of the differential as an alternating sum of face
1041	  restrictions (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}, the decl
1042	  \(\operatorname{sectionCech\_objD\_apply}\)) together with the per-coordinate
1043	  identification of each face restriction with the localisation coface
1044	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}) shows
1045	  \(e_{p+1} \circ \operatorname{objD} p = \operatorname{dDiff}^{\,p} \circ e_p\). The
1046	  horizontal exactness fed into the transport is the \(R\)-module exactness
1047	  \(H = \operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
1048	  Transporting \(H\) across the additive ladder yields the desired
1049	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the \(\operatorname{objD}\) maps, discharging the
1050	  hypothesis of the precursor.
1051	\end{proof}

1080	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)
1081	  the global sections of a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
1082	  R}\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) a spanning
1083	  family of the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) =
1084	  \top\) (Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}). The \emph{un-localised section
1085	  {\v C}ech module complex} \(D^\bullet\) is the cochain complex of \(R\)-modules
1086	  whose degree-\(p\) term is the product over multi-indices
1087	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of the away localisations
1088	  \[
1089	    D^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} M_{s_\sigma},
1090	    \qquad s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
1091	    \qquad M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}
1092	      \bigl(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\bigr)\, M,
1093	  \]
1094	  with differential \(d^p : D^p \to D^{p+1}\) the alternating sum
1095	  \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j\, \delta^j\) of the coface comparison maps \(\delta^j\), each of
1096	  which is the canonical away-localisation comparison
1097	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) sending a fraction to its image under
1098	  the further localisation at the omitted factor. The single-coefficient comparison
1099	  map and its localisation property are recorded by the \(\mathtt{dCoface}\) and
1100	  \(\mathtt{dCoeff}\) data and the differential by \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) (with action
1101	  \(\mathtt{dDiff\_apply}\)); the auxiliary \(\mathtt{spanIdx}\)/\(\mathtt{spanIdx\_spec}\)
1102	  select a witnessing index of the spanning relation.
1103	
1104	  The complex \(D^\bullet\) is tied to the section {\v C}ech complex of
1105	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and to its localisations by three
1106	  comparison families. First, the maps \(\mathtt{dToCech}\) identify each term
1107	  \(D^p\) with the degree-\(p\) section group
1108	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) of
1109	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} as a localised module
1110	  (\(\mathtt{dToCech\_isLocalizedModule}\)), intertwining the differentials
1111	  (\(\mathtt{cechCoface\_dToCech}\), \(\mathtt{dToCech\_comm}\)); the section-side
1112	  cofaces are \(\mathtt{cechCofaceLin}\) with action \(\mathtt{cechCoface\_apply}\).
1113	  Second, localising \(D^\bullet\) at a spanning element \(s_r\) reproduces the
1114	  \emph{localised} {\v C}ech complex: the localised differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1115	  (action \(\mathtt{locDiff\_apply}\)) agrees, via
1116	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\), with the dependent-coefficient combinatorial
1117	  differential, and its exactness is recorded as \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\). The
1118	  localisation comparison maps \(\mathtt{fLoc}\) (action \(\mathtt{fLoc\_apply}\))
1119	  exhibit each localised term as a localised module
1120	  (\(\mathtt{fLoc\_isLocalizedModule}\)) intertwining \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) with
1121	  \(\mathtt{locDiff}\) (\(\mathtt{locDiff\_fLoc}\), \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\)).
1122	  The key algebraic fact underlying all three families is the localisation
1123	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\): localising the away module \(M_{s_\sigma}\)
1124	  further at \(s_r\) gives \(M_{s_r s_\sigma}\). This is the keystone
1125	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\) (the composite of two away
1126	  localisations is again the away localisation at the product), with the cancellation
1127	  bookkeeping \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.Inverts.smul\_pow\_cancel}\). Thus localising
1128	  \(D^\bullet\) at \(s_r\) (an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.Away}\)) reproduces
1129	  the localised Čech complex of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, and
1130	  exactness of \(D^\bullet\) can be checked one localisation at a time.
1131	\end{definition}

1133	\begin{lemma}
1134	
1135	\leanok
1136	[Positive-degree exactness of the section {\v C}ech module complex]
1137	  \label{lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff_exact}
1139	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1140	  With the notation of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}, the
1141	  un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\) is exact in positive
1142	  degrees: for every \(p\),
1143	  \[
1144	    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(d^{p+1} : D^{p+1} \to D^{p+2},\;
1145	      d^{p+2} : D^{p+2} \to D^{p+3}\bigr).
1146	  \]
1147	  This is step~(a) of the affine vanishing: the node-by-node exactness of the
1148	  un-localised module complex, from which the homology vanishing of the section
1149	  {\v C}ech complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, step~(c)) is read off.
1150	\end{lemma}
1151	
1152	\begin{proof}
1153	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1154	  \leanok
1155	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1156	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1157	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1158	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1159	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1160	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1161	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1162	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1163	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1164	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1165	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that
1166	  localised differential is \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\), which is in turn the
1167	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial exactness transported along
1168	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\); the contracting homotopy at the fixed index
1169	  \(r\) (where \(s_r\) is invertible) furnishes \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\),
1170	  giving the localised exactness one localisation at a time. Reassembling over the
1171	  spanning family yields the un-localised exactness.
1172	\end{proof}

1334	  abstract section complex.

1336	  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
1337	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
1338	  extra-degeneracy packaging of the contracting homotopy is a documented dead end for
1339	  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
1340	  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete
1341	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
1342	  is used instead.
1343	\end{proof}

1345	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1346	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1347	
1348	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1349	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1350	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1351	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1352	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1353	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1354	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1355	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1356	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1357	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1358	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1359	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1360	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1361	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1362	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1363	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1364	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1365	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1366	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1367	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1368	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
1369	
1370	\begin{definition}
1371	
1372	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
1373	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1374	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,

1376	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverOpen,

1382	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1383	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
1384	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
1385	  % L1095--1135).
1386	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
1387	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1388	  % $$
1389	  % \ldots
1390	  % \to
1391	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
1392	  % \to
1393	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1394	  % \to
1395	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1396	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1397	  % $$
1398	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1399	  % and where the map
1400	  % $$
1401	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1402	  % \longrightarrow
1403	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1404	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1405	  % $$
1406	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1407	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1408	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1409	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1410	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1411	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1412	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1413	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1414	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1415	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1416	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1417	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1418	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1419	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1420	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1421	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1422	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1423	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1424	  \[
1425	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1426	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1427	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1428	  \]
1429	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1430	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1431	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1432	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1433	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1434	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1435	  \[
1436	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1437	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1438	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1439	  \]
1440	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1441	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1442	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1443	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1444	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1445	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1446	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1447	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1448	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1449	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1450	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1451	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1452	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1453	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1454	
1455	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1456	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1457	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1458	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1459	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1460	\end{definition}

1471	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L879--910).
1472	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Set
1473	  % $$
1474	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})

1477	  % \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
1478	  % $$
1479	  % This is an abelian group. ... We define
1480	  % $$
1481	  % d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1482	  % \longrightarrow
1483	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p + 1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1484	  % $$
1485	  % by the formula
1486	  % $$
1487	  % d(s)_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}
1488	  % =

1491	  % s_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}|_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}

1494	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
1495	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1496	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1497	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1498	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1499	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1500	  \[
1501	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1502	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1503	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1504	  \]
1505	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1506	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1507	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1508	  \[
1509	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1510	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1511	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1512	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1513	  \]
1514	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1515	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1516	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1517	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1518	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1519	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1520	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1521	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1522	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.
1523	\end{definition}

1592	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, def:section_cech_complex}
1593	  The free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules gives, composed with the
1594	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1595	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1596	  \[
1597	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(
1598	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),\, \mathcal{F}
1599	    \bigr)
1600	    \;\cong\;
1601	    \operatorname{Hom}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p},\,
1602	      \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1603	    \;\cong\;
1604	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1605	  \]
1606	  where the last group is the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open
1607	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,
1608	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_p, \mathcal{F}) =
1609	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =
1610	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\); the direct sum
1611	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) therefore represents the functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto
1612	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\). Under this
1613	  identification the \((-1)^j\) maps \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0
1614	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1615	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become
1616	  exactly the alternating-sum restriction differential of
1617	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so the two cochain
1618	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the
1619	  naturality of the free–forgetful adjunction and of Yoneda. The per-multi-index
1620	  bijection \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,
1621	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,
1622	  so it is promoted to an isomorphism of abelian groups (an \emph{additive}
1623	  equivalence); taking the product over multi-indices and assembling these degreewise
1624	  additive isomorphisms — which commute with the alternating-sum differentials — yields
1625	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.
1626	\end{proof}

1669	\begin{lemma}
1670	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1671	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1673	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1674	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1675	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1676	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1677	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1678	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).
1679	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended
1680	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1681	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1682	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1683	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1684	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1685	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1686	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1687	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1688	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1689	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1690	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1691	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1692	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1693	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1694	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1695	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1696	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1697	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1698	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1699	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1700	\end{lemma}
1701	
1702	\begin{proof}
1703	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1704	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1705	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1706	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1707	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1708	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1709	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1710	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1711	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1712	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1713	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1714	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1715	  quasi-isomorphism.
1716	\end{proof}

2040	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\). It is the chain/coproduct dual of the section-side
2041	  cochain identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}; isolating it
2042	  here gives the empty and nonempty cases (Lemmas~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}
2043	  and~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_nonempty}) a common, ready degreewise model.
2044	\end{lemma}
2045	
2046	\begin{proof}
2047	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2048	  \leanok
2049	  \emph{Degreewise.} By \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) of
2050	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} evaluation at \(V\) commutes with the
2051	  coproduct,
2052	  \((\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p) \cong \coprod_{\sigma :
2053	  \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0} (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}
2054	  (\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))\). Split the index set into the \(\sigma\) landing in
2055	  \(I_1(V)\) and the rest. Each summand with \(\sigma\) valued in \(I_1(V)\) (so
2056	  \(V \leq U_\sigma\)) is \(\cong \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) by
2057	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); each remaining summand is the zero
2058	  object by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\). Hence the coproduct
2059	  collapses to \(\coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1(V)} \mathcal{O}_X(V) =
2060	  C_p\).
2061	
2062	  \emph{Differential match.} The evaluated differential is \((\operatorname{eval} V)\)
2063	  applied to the alternating face sum
2064	  \(\operatorname{AlternatingFaceMapComplex.objD}(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}
2065	  \mathcal{U})\). The face \(\delta_i\) reindexes the \(\sigma\)-summand by
2066	  \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (the face action of
2067	  \(\operatorname{cechFreeSimplicial}\)); restricted to the
2068	  \(I_1\)-summands this is exactly the index-drop that the engine encodes, and the sign
2069	  \((-1)^i\) is supplied by \(\operatorname{objD}\). The match is verified on the
2070	  coproduct injections by \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\), reusing the
2071	  naturality of \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\); summing with signs
2072	  identifies the evaluated differential with
2073	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\). This is the hard content of the
2074	  identification and the chain/coproduct dual of the cochain coface match in
2075	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}.
2076	
2077	  \emph{The three layers commuted past.} The degreewise iso is the \(3\)-fold
2078	  composite
2079	  \[
2080	    (\operatorname{eval} V)(K(\mathcal{U})_p)
2081	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\;}
2082	      \coprod_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \mathcal{U}.I_0}
2083	        (\operatorname{eval} V)(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma))
2084	      \xrightarrow{\;\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\;}
2085	      \coprod_{V \leq U_\sigma} \mathcal{O}_X(V)
2086	      \xrightarrow{\;(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \ldots)^{-1}\;}
2087	      C_p,
2088	  \]
2089	  whose layers are: (1) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_X}\) = the comparison
2090	  \(\operatorname{Limits.PreservesCoproduct.iso}\) expressing that evaluation at
2091	  \(V\) commutes with the coproduct \(\coprod\); (2) \(\operatorname{cechFreeEvalDropZeros}\),
2092	  which kills the summands with \(V \not\leq U_\sigma\) (these evaluate to zero objects
2093	  by \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_isZero\_of\_not\_le}\)); (3)
2094	  \((\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.whiskerEquiv}\ \operatorname{survivingEquiv}\
2095	  (\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\, \ldots))^{-1}\), which reindexes the
2096	  surviving summands by \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) of
2097	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} and identifies each evaluated summand with the
2098	  single coefficient \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\) via \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\).
2099	
2100	  \emph{Naturality past each layer.} The reindex \(\operatorname{survivingEquiv}\) is
2101	  natural in the face map \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\):
2102	  a surviving index \(\sigma\) (i.e.\ \(V \leq U_\sigma\), equivalently
2103	  \(V \leq \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\, \mathcal{U}\, \sigma\) by
2104	  \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\)) is carried to a surviving
2105	  \(\sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\) (still \(V \leq U_{\sigma \circ
2106	  \operatorname{succAbove}\, i}\), since dropping a coordinate only weakens the meet),
2107	  so the drop-zeros layer (2) and the whisker layer (3) commute with every face. On a
2108	  surviving summand the evaluated transition \(\operatorname{freeYoneda}.\mathrm{map}
2109	  (\operatorname{homOfLE}\, \ldots)\) collapses to the identity under
2110	  \(\operatorname{freeYonedaEval\_iso\_of\_le}\); hence each face \(\delta_i\) matches the
2111	  engine reindexing \(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\)
2112	  summand-for-summand, and summing with the \(\operatorname{objD}\) signs \((-1)^i\)
2113	  identifies the evaluated alternating-face differential with
2114	  \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) (\(= \) the chain transpose of
2115	  \(\operatorname{combDifferential}\)).
2116	
2117	  \emph{The genuine difficulty: variance.} The free \emph{chain} differential
2118	  \emph{lowers} \(\mathrm{Fin}\)-arity (\(\sigma \mapsto \sigma \circ
2119	  \operatorname{Fin.succAbove} i\), faces), whereas
2120	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combDifferential}\) \emph{raises} it (it inserts an
2121	  index, summing over \(i \in I_1\)). The two are reconciled because on the finite
2122	  biproduct \(\coprod = \prod\) the insertion/deletion adjunction makes the index-drop
2123	  chain differential the transpose of the index-raise cochain differential; this
2124	  transpose is precisely what \(\operatorname{cechEngineD}\) of
2125	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex} packages, and verifying the commutation amounts to
2126	  the injectionwise check \(\operatorname{Limits.Sigma.hom\_ext}\) outlined above.
2127	\end{proof}

2131	[Evaluated free complex over an open meeting no cover member]
2132	  \label{lem:cech_free_eval_empty}

2134	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeEval_isZero_of_isEmpty,
2135	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverStructurePresheaf_eval_isZero_of_isEmpty,
2136	    AlgebraicGeometry.isZero_homology_of_isZero_X}
2137	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise, lem:free_cech_engine}
2138	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2139	  % (proof, the empty case) (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex,
2140	  % L1228--1230).
2141	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $I_1 = \emptyset$, then the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W) = 0$
2142	  % so there is nothing to prove."
2143	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2144	  (empty case).}
2145	  With the notation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}, suppose
2146	  \(I_1(V) = \varnothing\), i.e.\ \(V\) is contained in no cover member \(U_i\). Then
2147	  the evaluated augmented complex is identically zero: every degree-\(p\) term
2148	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) is the empty
2149	  direct sum \(0\) (there is no map \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \varnothing\)) and the
2150	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\). Consequently the
2151	  evaluation at \(V\) of \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\) is a morphism of zero
2152	  complexes, which is a quasi-isomorphism (both sides have vanishing homology). The Lean
2153	  bricks are \(\operatorname{cechFreeEval\_isZero\_of\_isEmpty}\) (each evaluated source
2154	  term is a zero object, via the coproduct-of-zeros helper
2155	  \(\operatorname{isZero\_sigma\_of\_forall\_isZero}\) of
2156	  Lemma~\ref{lem:free_cech_engine}),
2157	  \(\operatorname{coverStructurePresheaf\_eval\_isZero\_of\_isEmpty}\) (the evaluated
2158	  augmentation target is zero), and the generic
2159	  \(\operatorname{isZero\_homology\_of\_isZero\_X}\) (a short complex with zero middle
2160	  term has vanishing homology), which together force every
2161	  \(\operatorname{homologyMap}\) to be a map of zero objects.
2162	\end{lemma}
2163	
2164	\begin{proof}
2165	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}
2166	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise} each evaluated term is
2167	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\); when
2168	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\) the index type \(\mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) is empty, so the
2169	  term is the zero object, and likewise \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\). A
2170	  morphism whose source and target complexes are both zero (so all their homology
2171	  objects are zero, \(\operatorname{IsZero}\)) is a quasi-isomorphism; in Lean this is
2172	  immediate from \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\) since each
2173	  \(\operatorname{homologyMap}\) is a map of zero objects, hence an isomorphism.
2174	\end{proof}

2244	  % engine-complex level as `cechEnginePrepend_spec` (with positive-degree exactness
2245	  % packaged as `cechEngineD_exact`), both pinned by lem:cech_engine_complex. This

2250	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2251	  % (proof, the $dh + hd = \mathrm{id}$ computation) (read from

2253	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Then we compute
2254	  % $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p} +

2256	  % (-1)^j (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) s_{i_1 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}

2258	  % which is equal to $s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ as desired."
2259	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
2260	  (the contracting identity).}
2261	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy}
2262	  (\(I_1(V) \neq \varnothing\), \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} \in I_1\) fixed), the transported
2263	  prepend homotopy \(h\) satisfies the contracting identity
2264	  \[
2265	    d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}
2266	  \]
2267	  on the augmented evaluated complex
2268	  \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1(V) \to K(\mathcal{U})_0(V) \to
2269	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) \to 0\) (including the degree-\(0\)/degree-\((-1)\)
2270	  augmentation term). Here \(d\) is the alternating-sum {\v C}ech differential of
2271	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_eval_sectionwise}.

2274	\begin{proof}
2275	  \uses{lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy, lem:free_cech_engine}
2276	  This is the same alternating-sum cancellation as
2277	  \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.combHomotopy\_spec}\)
2278	  (\(\operatorname{combDifferential}(\operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, t) +
2279	  \operatorname{combHomotopy} r\, (\operatorname{combDifferential} t) = t\)),
2280	  specialised to coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Expanding
2281	  \((dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\): the \(j = 0\) term of \(dh\) prepends and then
2282	  removes \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), returning \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\); every remaining term
2283	  of \(dh\) cancels in pairs against the corresponding term of \(hd\) (the
2284	  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) of the differential), because removing an index
2285	  \(j \geq 1\) after prepending \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) equals prepending
2286	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) after removing index \(j-1\), with opposite signs. The net
2287	  result is \(s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), i.e.\ \(dh + hd = \mathrm{id}\); the index
2288	  bookkeeping is the \(\operatorname{FreeCechEngine.cons\_comp\_succAbove\_succ}\) /

2448	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]
2449	  \label{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
2450	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.injective_of_adjoint}
2451	  \mathlibok
2452	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2453	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Preadditive.Injective.Basic}).}
2454	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
2455	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
2456	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.
2457	\end{lemma}

2459	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion of sheaves of modules]
2460	  \label{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}

2462	  \mathlibok
2463	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2464	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
2465	  For the identity ring map \(\alpha = \mathbf{1}\) over a scheme \(X\),
2466	  sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion
2467	  \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
2468	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2469	  \[

2477	\end{lemma}

2547	  \label{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}

2552	  contravariant hom functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I) =
2553	  \operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,I : \mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}} \to
2554	  \mathrm{Ab}\) is exact: it is automatically left exact (preserves finite limits), and
2555	  injectivity of \(I\) makes it carry epimorphisms to epimorphisms, so it preserves
2556	  finite colimits (\(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda\_obj\_preservesFiniteColimits\_of\_injective}\))
2557	  and hence preserves homology. Consequently \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, I)\) carries
2558	  quasi-isomorphisms of chain complexes in \(\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{op}}\) to
2559	  quasi-isomorphisms of the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-cochain complexes
2560	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\)). Both statements
2561	  are for a general abelian category, the right generality for reuse in
2562	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The mechanism --- a right adjoint / exact
2563	  contravariant functor preserves the relevant exactness --- is the same homological
2564	  principle behind Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint}.

2572	  epimorphisms. Combined with the automatic preservation of finite limits, the
2573	  short-exact-sequence criterion
2574	  \(\operatorname{Functor.preservesFiniteColimits\_iff\_forall\_exact\_map\_and\_epi}\)
2575	  shows it preserves finite colimits, hence (being also left exact) it has
2576	  \(\operatorname{PreservesHomology}\) by
2577	  \(\operatorname{Functor.preservesHomologyOfExact}\). A homology-preserving functor

2607	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{U} : U =
2608	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be any open covering, and let \(\mathcal{I}\) be an
2609	  \emph{injective} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the {\v C}ech cohomology of

2611	  \[

2614	      \mathcal{I}(U) & p = 0, \\

2616	    \end{cases}

2621	% object in
2622	% the category $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ (for example since
2623	% sheafification is an exact left adjoint to the inclusion functor,
2624	% using Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-adjoint-preserve-injectives}).

2626	% (or its proof) to see the result."
2627	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective presheaf of
2628	% $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.

2638	% quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
2639	% the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
2640	% that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
2641	% $i > 0$."

2643	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_op_identification, lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso, lem:hom_into_injective_exact}
2644	  The argument has two independent parts.
2645	
2646	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
2647	  left adjoint to the inclusion
2648	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
2649	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
2650	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
2651	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
2652	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
2653	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
2654	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
2655	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
2656	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2657	
2658	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
2659	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
2660	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
2661	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
2662	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
2663	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
2664	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
2665	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
2666	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
2667	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
2668	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
2669	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
2670	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
2671	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
2672	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\).
2673	
2674	  \emph{Categorical assembly.} Concretely the exactness is produced by the
2675	  opposite-transport bridge. The augmentation \(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}
2676	  \mathcal{U}\) is a quasi-isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso});
2677	  taking its opposite via the \(\operatorname{HomologicalComplex.op}\) machinery and
2678	  applying \(\operatorname{preadditiveYoneda}.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{I}\) keeps it a
2679	  quasi-isomorphism, since \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as a presheaf of modules
2680	  (Part 1) and \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) then preserves homology
2681	  (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_map\_preadditiveYoneda\_of\_injective}\),
2682	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hom_into_injective_exact}). Transporting source and target of this
2683	  mapped-opposite quasi-isomorphism across the opposite-transport isos
2684	  \(\operatorname{homCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2685	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_op_identification}) and
2686	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechComplexMapOpIso}\)
2687	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_complex_mapop_iso}) identifies it with the augmentation
2688	  of the section {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
2689	  \mathcal{I})\) onto the mapped image of the degree-\(0\)-concentrated target
2690	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\). The latter has vanishing homology in positive
2691	  degrees, so the section {\v C}ech complex does too. Hence
2692	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
2693	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
2694	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
2695	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
2696	\end{proof}

2768	  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) and (b) each restriction \(s|_{U_i}\) lifts to a section
2769	  \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\); both can be arranged simultaneously because the
2770	  coverings with property (a) are cofinal and (b) holds on any sufficiently fine
2771	  cover (surjectivity of \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}\) is a local condition).

2773	  \[

2788	\section{Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the structure sheaf}
2789	
2790	The two vanishing lemmas below speak about the \emph{absolute} sheaf cohomology
2791	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module over an open \(U\). Their
2792	dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
2793	identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
2794	sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
2795	Ext of the structure sheaf, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf; the
2796	following definition fixes that realization, and the Mathlib dependency anchors after it
2797	pin the exact results it rests on.
2798	
2799	\begin{definition}
2800	[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the structure sheaf]
2801	  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
2802	  \uses{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib, lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib,
2803	    lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib, lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib,
2804	    lem:hasext_standard_mathlib, lem:modules_restrict_functor_mathlib}
2805	  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
2806	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
2807	  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Write
2808	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U\) for the restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\) along \(j\) — the image of
2809	  \(\mathcal{F}\) under the Mathlib restriction functor \(j^\ast :
2810	  X.\mathrm{Modules} \to U.\mathrm{Modules}\) of
2811	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_restrict_functor_mathlib} — and \(\mathcal{O}_U\) for the
2812	  structure sheaf of \(U\) regarded as a module over itself. The \emph{absolute sheaf
2813	  cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of the structure
2814	  sheaf in the abelian category \(U.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_U)\) of
2815	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules:
2816	  \[
2817	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_U)}
2818	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}|_U\bigr),
2819	    \qquad p \geq 0,
2820	  \]
2821	  equivalently \(\operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2822	  \mathcal{F})\). Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
2823	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on the abelian category
2824	  \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\); the \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available
2825	  unconditionally by Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts
2826	  the downstream dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding
2827	  Mathlib results:
2828	  \begin{enumerate}
2829	  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
2830	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}|_U) \cong
2831	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}|_U) = \Gamma(U,
2832	    \mathcal{F})\), by the degree-zero Ext comparison
2833	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); so \(H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2834	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2835	  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
2836	    injective module \(\mathcal{J}\), one has \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-,
2837	    \mathcal{J}) = 0\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Placing an
2838	    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\) in that second-argument role
2839	    (via the \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) form over \(\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)) yields
2840	    \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly, with no appeal to restriction preserving
2841	    injectives.
2842	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
2843	    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) of modules and the \emph{fixed} first
2844	    argument \(\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact sequence
2845	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of the
2846	    groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext class
2847	    of the extension.
2848	  \end{enumerate}
2849	\end{definition}
2850

2852	[Ext bifunctor in an abelian category]
2853	  \label{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}
2854	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext}
2855	  \mathlibok
2856	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2857	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2858	  For an abelian category \(C\) with a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure and objects \(X, Y
